title: Cognitive Load & Miller's Law
summary: Working memory holds 4±1 chunks at a time. Every element on screen that requires interpretation consumes a chunk. When overloaded, users freeze, leave, or make errors.
rules: Break long forms into steps — one decision per screen
Use progressive disclosure: show advanced options only on demand
Remove any element that doesn't directly serve the user's current goal
dos: Use chunking: group phone numbers as (555) 123-4567, not 5551234567
Show only the fields relevant to the current step
Use defaults intelligently to reduce required decisions
donts: Don't show all configuration options at once
Don't use jargon that requires mental translation
Don't make users remember information from a previous screen

title: F-Pattern & Reading Patterns
summary: Eye-tracking studies show users scan in an F-shape on content-heavy pages: full first line, partial second line, then left edge. The right side of the screen below the fold is largely ignored.
rules: Place the most important content in the top-left quadrant
Front-load key information in headings and first sentences
Use left-aligned text for scannable lists
Break F-pattern with images, callouts, or visual interrupts
dos: Put your primary CTA in the top-right or end of the first line
Use bold or callout boxes to interrupt the F scan
Front-load bullet points with the key word
donts: Don't put critical information in the right column (it will be missed)
Don't bury the lead — lead with the most important point
Don't use long dense paragraphs — users scan, not read

title: Progressive Disclosure
summary: Show only what's needed for the current task. Reveal complexity progressively as the user's intent becomes clear.
rules: Show 'Advanced' or 'More options' toggles instead of always-visible settings
Onboarding should ask minimum required info — get more detail over time
Expandable sections work better than tabs for settings pages
dos: Use a wizard pattern for complex multi-step flows
Defer optional configuration to after the user achieves first value
Reveal contextual options (edit, delete) only on hover/focus
donts: Don't show power-user options to new users on their first visit
Don't ask for information before it's needed
Don't collapse information that users need frequently
